perturbation.original_text: “ Confidential Information ” means any information, technical data or know-how, software, data and analysis, including without limitation information relating to or including released or unreleased products and services, product development efforts, the marketing or promotion of any product, business policies or practices, business plans and forecasts, financial results, potential transactions and business combinations, the fact the parties are in discussions with each other and any information related to those discussions, and other information relating to the business, operations and affairs of the disclosing party, whether conveyed in writing or orally, regardless of whether marked confidential or proprietary, the manner or form in which it is provided, or whether tangible or intangible, furnished by or on behalf of the disclosing party to the receiving party or its Associates following the date of this agreement in connection with the evaluation of a potential Transaction (including, without limitation, any information that the disclosing party notifies the receiving party has been made available to the disclosing party or its Associates by third parties and that the disclosing party has an obligation to such third party to keep confidential, as long as the receiving party is deemed to have been notified that the disclosing party has an obligation to keep confidential the terms of its contracts with third parties).
perturbation.changed_text: “ Confidential Information ” means any information, technical data or know-how, software, data and analysis, including without limitation information relating to or including released or unreleased products and services, product development efforts, the marketing or promotion of any product, business policies or practices, business plans and forecasts, financial results, potential transactions and business combinations, the fact the parties are in discussions with each other and any information related to those discussions, and other information relating to the business, operations and affairs of the disclosing party, whether conveyed in writing or orally, regardless of whether marked confidential or proprietary, the manner or form in which it is provided, or whether tangible or intangible, furnished by or on behalf of the disclosing party to the receiving party or its Associates following the date of this agreement in connection with the evaluation of a potential Transaction (excluding, without limitation, any information that the disclosing party notifies the receiving party has been made available to the disclosing party or its Associates by third parties and that the disclosing party has an obligation to such third party to keep confidential, as long as the receiving party is deemed to have been notified that the disclosing party has an obligation to keep confidential the terms of its contracts with third parties).
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'including' to 'excluding', the definition of 'Confidential Information' now potentially excludes information the disclosing party is obligated to keep confidential due to agreements with third parties. This undermines the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement and could lead to the unintentional disclosure of protected information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act protects trade secrets, which are a subset of confidential information. By excluding information the disclosing party is obligated to keep confidential to third parties, the modified definition weakens the protection of potential trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the intent and provisions of the EEA if such information meets the definition of a trade secret under 18 U.S. Code § 1839.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text redefines 'Confidential Information' to exclude data the disclosing party is obligated to keep confidential due to agreements with third parties, whereas the original text included this information. The Law Explanation suggests this alteration could weaken the protection of potential trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the Economic Espionage Act (EEA) if such information meets the definition of a trade secret under 18 U.S. Code § 1839. Scraped Law Snippet 2 provides a link to the definitions within 18 USC 1839, further suggesting a potential conflict as the revised definition could undermine the protection of trade secrets depending on whether the excluded information meets the legal definition. This undermines the purpose of an NDA.

perturbation.original_text: Each party is aware, and will take all reasonable precautions to prevent any trading in securities of the other party, including advising its Associates who are informed of the matters that are the subject of this letter agreement, of the restrictions imposed by the United States securities laws on the purchase or sale of securities by any person who has received material, non-public information of the issuer of such securities and on the communication of such information to any other person when it is reasonably foreseeable that such other person is likely to purchase or sell such securities in reliance upon such information, and directing such Associates to comply with those restrictions.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party is aware, and will take precautions to prevent any trading in securities of the other party, including advising its Associates who are informed of the matters that are the subject of this letter agreement, of the restrictions imposed by the United States securities laws on the purchase or sale of securities by any person who has received material, non-public information of the issuer of such securities and on the communication of such information to any other person when it is reasonably foreseeable that such other person is likely to purchase or sell such securities in reliance upon such information, and directing such Associates to comply with those restrictions.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'all reasonable' makes the required level of precaution ambiguous. 'Precautions' are not clearly defined.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 prohibit the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Failing to take 'all reasonable precautions' to prevent insider trading could be seen as a failure to diligently prevent the misuse of material non-public information, potentially violating these provisions. The 'reasonable' standard sets a benchmark of expected behavior.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'all reasonable precautions,' whereas the changed text removes 'all reasonable,' thus weakening the standard of care required to prevent insider trading. The Law Explanation correctly states that Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 prohibit manipulative and deceptive devices in the purchase or sale of securities. The explanation further notes that failing to take 'all reasonable precautions' could be seen as a failure to diligently prevent the misuse of material non-public information, potentially violating these provisions. 15 USC 78j, provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, also prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices. By removing the phrase 'all reasonable,' the contract now provides less protection against insider trading, potentially contradicting the prohibitions in Section 10(b), Rule 10b-5, and 15 USC 78j.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and (except as provided in Section 10) shall continue for a period of twelve (12) months following the date of this agreement. Nothing herein is intended to limit or abridge the protection of trade secrets under applicable trade secrets law, and the protection of trade secrets by the receiving party shall be maintained as such until they fall into the public domain.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and (except as provided in Section 10) shall continue for a period of twelve (12) months following the date of this agreement. The receiving party is obligated to protect Confidential Information even if it is a trade secret, but only for twelve months after the contract date.
perturbation.explanation: This directly contradicts the statement that 'Nothing herein is intended to limit or abridge the protection of trade secrets under applicable trade secrets law', and limits the protection of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret and provide economic value. The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), adopted in many states, provides legal recourse for misappropriation of trade secrets. By limiting the protection of even trade secrets to 12 months, this clause directly contradicts the UTSA and applicable trade secret laws which protect trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain secret.
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original contract and the underlying principle of trade secret law. The original contract states that the protection of trade secrets is not limited by the agreement. However, the changed text limits the protection of confidential information, including trade secrets, to only twelve months, which directly undermines the potentially indefinite protection that trade secrets are afforded under applicable trade secret laws, as described in the Law Explanation. Since the Perturbation Explanation indicates a clear conflict, and the Law Explanation states that the changed text contradicts the UTSA and applicable trade secret laws, the 'YES' determination is appropriate.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement will be binding upon and inure to the benefit of each party and its Associates and their respective heirs, successors and permitted assigns. Any assignment of this agreement without the prior written consent of the other party shall be null and void.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement will be binding upon and inure to the benefit of each party and its Associates and their respective heirs, successors and assigns. Any assignment of this agreement without the prior written consent of the other party shall be valid.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'null and void' to 'valid', an assignment can happen even without consent.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Delaware contract law generally respects freedom of contract, changes that fundamentally undermine the agreement's intent (like allowing assignment without consent when the original agreement explicitly forbids it) are challengeable. Section 2704 outlines the basic contractual principles that would be challenged in such a case. The validity of an anti-assignment clause is part of this principle. The key is determining if the clause is ambiguous (see Delaware law).

(a) All bonds, specialties, and notes in writing, payable to any person, or order, or assigns, may be assigned, or indorsed, and the assignee, or indorsee, his or her executors, administrators, or assigns, may again assign, or indorse the same, as often as desired. perturbation.justification: The original text states that any assignment without prior written consent is 'null and void', effectively prohibiting such assignments. The changed text replaces 'null and void' with 'valid', directly contradicting the original restriction and allowing assignments without consent. The Law Explanation indicates that Delaware law respects freedom of contract, but changes undermining the agreement's intent are challengeable. While the scraped law snippet 1 discusses the assignment of bonds, specialties, and notes in writing under 6 Del. C. Â§ 2702, this snippet does not directly address the validity of anti-assignment clauses within broader contracts. The changed text directly opposes the original intent of requiring consent, making assignments permissible without it. This constitutes a legal contradiction because it reverses a key contractual protection.
